trouble_description:
At 14:09, OG.1 on Busbar 1 briefly lost voltage due to an auto-reclose event at GSK.3. Because of the auto-reclose, the voltage loss on OG.1 was momentary, and the supply was quickly restored from GSK.3.
Upon further investigation, it was found that GSK.3 auto-reclosed because the load current briefly reached 444 A, which exceeded the OCR setting of OG.1 at 402 A (however, the OCR of OG.1 did not operate and the OG.1 circuit breaker remained in the closed position).
The load on Busbar 1 reached 13.8 MW, which exceeded the setpoint limit of 12 MW. The setpoint for Busbar 1 had been increased earlier from 10 MW to 12 MW at 12:30.

trouble_description: The PCS experienced an anomaly in which it suddenly performed charging or discharging operations without any command from the SmartLogger or Univers.
action: Based on the analysis of the device log and communication log from the PCS, configuration changes were made to the PCS via Enspire. The LVRT, HVRT, overfrequency derating, underfrequency rise power, and microgrid compatibility settings were set to "disable." Additionally, the automatic SOC calibration feature was also disabled.

Inverter 512 was found to have a Communication Disconnected status and a Grid Loss alarm appeared on the Enspire system. An inspection was carried out on the MCCB of Inverter 512 at the LV-B STS 5 panel, and it was discovered that the MCCB had tripped. Subsequently, the MCCB’s current setting (Ir) was adjusted from 0.7 × In to 0.8 × In. The issue was resolved by switching the MCCB of Inverter 512 back on. After the MCCB was reactivated, Inverter 512 resumed normal operation. It was also found that the labeling/tagging on the MCCB of Inverter 512 was incorrect (labeled as MCCB Inverter 502 in the actual condition), and the label has since been corrected.

Inverter 501 experienced a Shutdown Fault, and a DC Terminal Temperature Abnormal alarm appeared on the Enspire system. The inverter was then shut down via the AC isolator box and DC switch 1, as the alarm was triggered on MPPT 3. Subsequently, current measurement was conducted and found to be normal. Voltage checks also showed normal results. It was discovered that the MC4 connector of PV string 9 (DC negative) to the inverter was loose and slightly dirty. The MC4 connection was then tightened and cleaned. Afterward, the AC isolator box and DC switch were turned back on. The inverter resumed normal operation.

Reading condition on SCADA was always at its last state and Enspire could not be accessed. Further tracing revealed a suspected fault in the pyranometer power cable at Weather Station 5. This suspicion was confirmed when unplugging the cable allowed the SmartLogger and SmartModule at STS 2 to power on again. As a temporary solution, the pyranometer power cable at Weather Station 5 was left disconnected, allowing STS 2 to be monitored again via SCADA.

trouble_description: At 14:11, OG.1 on Busbar 1 experienced a momentary voltage loss due to an auto reclose event at GSK.3. Previously, OG.2 had already been in an outage condition (CB Open) since 12:20 due to a fault at GSK.2. Since GSK.3 performed an auto reclose, the voltage loss on OG.1 was brief, and it quickly resumed receiving power supply from GSK.3. Upon further investigation, it was found that the auto reclose at GSK.3 occurred because the PLTS generation reached 14.638 MW, clearly exceeding the limit of 12 MW. This happened because the setpoint for STS 2 could not be adjusted, resulting in the actual total setpoint for Busbar 1 exceeding 12 MW. The inability to adjust STS 2's setpoint was caused by a loss of communication on STS 2 starting at 12:20. As a mitigation measure, the setpoints of STS 1, 3, 4, and 5 were lowered to maintain the Busbar 1 total setpoint within the 12 MW limit, considering that STS 2 remained unadjustable. Upon inspection, it was found that the SmartLogger and SmartModule of STS 2 were not turning on, even though they were still receiving a single-phase 220 V power supply from the STS distribution panel. As a result, STS 2's setpoint remained unchangeable (last recorded at 4568 kW).
action: Normal shutdown, Reset alarm relay, Close CB, and Normal start

trouble_description: The power cable of the pyranometer is suspected to be faulty.
action: The initial troubleshooting was performed by connecting a 12 V external DC power supply unit (PSU) to the old pyranometer; however, the voltage measurement indicated an abnormal condition, leading to the conclusion that the old pyranometer was damaged. Subsequently, the old pyranometer was replaced with the RSI-01 pyranometer from WS4. After reinstalling the power and communication cables, the PSU supply voltage was measured at 12.22 VDC. The pyranometer configuration was then verified using the Hukseflux Sensor Manager software, and the communication address was adjusted to match the settings in Enspire. Once the communication cable was reconnected to the smart module, verification in Enspire/SCADA showed that the device status turned green and the parameter data was successfully read.

trouble_description: Power output on busbar 2 exceeds the predefined setpoint
action: Based on the results of the investigation, the incident was related to the BESS, which is not included in the parameters controlled by the busbar 2 setpoint, as the busbar 2 setpoint only regulates the MW output from STS 6–10 and does not cover BESS. An email request has been sent to Univers to adjust the BESS smoothing discharge algorithm, so that the power output of Busbar 2 (Panel J14 Outgoing Busbar 2) does not exceed the setpoint of 30,030 kW.

trouble_description: An overcurrent trip occurred on the J10 GI 20 kV Solar Power Plant (Substation) panel, with a recorded current of 1007 A, exceeding the OCR J10 GI pickup setting of 1000 A. This incident was caused by busbar 2 production at J14 reaching 35 MW, which exceeded the defined busbar 2 set point of 30 MW. Further inspection revealed that the BESS was discharging approximately 7 MW while the STS 6–10 units were producing around 28 MW, resulting in a total J14 output of 28 + 7 = 35 MW. This issue is related to the BESS smoothing algorithm, which has not yet accounted for the maximum busbar 2 set point limit of 30 MW.
action: Normal shutdown, Reset alarm relay, Close CB, and Normal start

trouble_description: Inspection checklist fire hydrant and pump for BAST. During the fire suppression test using the hydrant pillar built in phase 2 (located near the BESS), water could not flow, preventing the extinguishing process from being carried out.
action: Conducting inspections in accordance with the NFPA 25 checklist for hydrants and pumps. Based on the initial investigation and the inspection of the design and condition of the fire hydrant pipeline, it was found that the reason water could not flow to the hydrant pillars built in phase 2 (located near the BESS) was that the water flow was blocked by a double check valve located beside the siamese connection. we recommend relocating the siamese connection, double check valve, and gate valve to the end of the fire hydrant pipeline to ensure that the water flow from the pump can reach the end of the line normally and supply all hydrant pillars.

Cleaning was carried out on the insulators and components of the MV OG.5 cubicle, followed by insulation resistance testing on the OG.5 CB. The test results showed that the insulation resistance values remain low. The preliminary inspection results of the insulation resistance testing on the 20 kV Outgoing 5 cubicle equipment after cleaning are as follows:

1. The insulation resistance test results of the CB are still below the acceptance criteria, with all test values below 1,000 MΩ.
2. The insulation resistance test results of the busbar insulators are still below the acceptance criteria.
3. The insulation resistance test results of the CT show that the insulation resistance value of phase S is lower than the other phases.
4. The insulation resistance test results of the Voltage Presence Indicating System (VPIS) show that phase S has a short circuit to ground.

trouble_description: PV strings and cable were found to be potentially fault at WB09-INV16-ST18, WB09-INV16-ST19 and WB09-INV16-ST20
action: As a corrective action, the burned DC string cables on WB09-INV16 String 18 and String 19 were replaced. Upon completion of the replacement, insulation resistance testing and voltage measurements were performed on each string to verify that the insulation condition and operating voltage met the required specifications before the inverter was returned to service. Subsequently, DC Switch 2 was kept in the OFF position to isolate the affected circuit that remained under observation, after which the inverter was normalized and returned to operation while its operating parameters and overall condition continued to be closely monitored to ensure safe and stable operation
